Softpowers Spec

Softpowers Spec

tmux Session Sidebar Plugin Design

Table of contents

  1. Problem and goals
  2. V1 scope and non-goals
  3. Chosen solution
  4. User flows and keybindings
  5. Plugin architecture
  6. Configuration and state
  7. Error handling and edge cases
  8. Testing and verification
  9. Future extensions
Status
Approved for planning
Repository target
/home/brice/projects/tmux-session-sidebar
Chosen v1 pattern
An on-demand real left pane sidebar reopened in the current window
Dependencies
tmux 3.6+ and shell are required; fzf is optional but recommended

Problem and goals

The plugin should make tmux session management feel project-centric instead of server-centric. The main use case is working across multiple projects at the same time, with one tmux session per project, each session holding its own windows and panes.

The design favors a real left pane over a popup because the user wants a sidebar that feels native to tmux. The plugin must still switch sessions inside the same terminal client, so the workflow stays fast and does not require detaching, reattaching, or opening another terminal.

  • Open a left-side management pane from the current tmux window.
  • Switch sessions in the same tmux client with minimal friction.
  • Create project-backed sessions quickly from a directory picker.
  • Create ad-hoc sessions without forcing a project path.
  • Rename and kill sessions without leaving the sidebar workflow.
  • Stay TPM-friendly and lightweight enough to install with shell scripts.

V1 scope and non-goals

V1 should be small, reliable, and easy to install. It should solve the daily workflow first and avoid building a full terminal IDE or a persistent tmux control plane.

In scope

  • Open and toggle a real left sidebar pane with split-window -hb.
  • List tmux sessions and mark the current session clearly.
  • Switch to a selected session with switch-client -t ....
  • Create a session from a chosen project directory.
  • Create an ad-hoc session by name.
  • Rename and kill sessions from the same sidebar flow.
  • Read configuration from tmux user options.
  • Fallback to tmux prompts when fzf is unavailable.

Out of scope

  • A truly global sidebar that survives session switches without being reopened.
  • Automatic session restore, templates, or layout persistence.
  • A long-running daemon, external database, or background service.
  • A rich standalone TUI beyond shell scripts plus optional fzf.
  • Cross-client state synchronization beyond tmux's native client semantics.

Chosen solution

The selected v1 approach is an ephemeral left-pane controller. A TPM entry script binds a toggle key, reads tmux user options, and launches scripts/open-sidebar.sh. That script opens a real left pane in the current window and runs scripts/sidebar.sh inside it.

The sidebar is a controller, not a persistent layout object. tmux clients are attached to one session at a time, so the sidebar cannot exist as a global pane across all sessions. Instead, the plugin embraces the native model: switch the current client with switch-client -t SESSION, then let the user reopen the sidebar on demand in the new session.

When fzf is installed, the sidebar uses it as the primary picker and action surface. The script feeds fzf a live session list, highlights the current session, and uses modified action keys for create, rename, kill, and close so ordinary letters remain available for fuzzy search. Without fzf, the same actions fall back to tmux prompts and a simpler menu loop that can use plain single-letter commands.

This approach was chosen because it preserves the real-pane feel, keeps installation simple for TPM users, and avoids the fragility of hook-heavy attempts to force a universal sticky sidebar.

User flows and keybindings

Primary entrypoint

The default keybinding is prefix + B. Pressing it toggles the sidebar in the current tmux window. If the current window already contains a marked sidebar pane, the plugin closes that pane instead of opening a duplicate.

Sidebar actions

ActionDefault interactionResult
Open or close sidebarprefix + BToggle the left pane controller in the current window.
Switch sessionEnter on the selected sessionRun switch-client -t SESSION for the current client.
Create project sessionAlt+n in fzf mode; n in fallback modeOpen a project picker, derive a default session name from the directory, create the session, then switch to it.
Create ad-hoc sessionAlt+a in fzf mode; a in fallback modePrompt for a session name and create a non-project session.
Rename sessionAlt+r in fzf mode; r in fallback modePrompt for a new session name and rename the selected or current session.
Kill sessionAlt+x in fzf mode; x in fallback modeConfirm, then kill the selected session unless it is the last remaining session.
Close sidebarEsc in fzf mode; q or Esc in fallback modeExit the controller and close the sidebar pane.

When the fzf path is active, modified action keys are part of the public UX. That trade-off keeps normal letters available for fuzzy search instead of turning the search prompt into a fragile command surface. The non-fzf fallback loop can use plain single-letter commands because it does not share input with a fuzzy finder.

Project-backed create flow

The primary create flow is project-first. The plugin reads a colon-separated list of roots from a tmux option, searches those directories, and passes the results to fzf when available. The chosen directory becomes the session working directory, and the default session name comes from the directory basename after light sanitization for tmux-safe naming.

If the derived session name already exists, v1 should switch to the existing session by default instead of silently creating variants. A later version can add a richer collision prompt, but v1 should keep the flow predictable.

Plugin architecture

The implementation should stay shell-first and keep file boundaries clear. Small focused scripts are easier to test, easier to debug, and easier for TPM users to inspect.

PathResponsibility
tmux-session-sidebar.tmuxTPM entrypoint. Defines defaults, reads tmux options, and binds the toggle key.
scripts/open-sidebar.shDetects whether the current window already has a sidebar pane, opens or closes it, and launches the controller script with the right width and target client context.
scripts/sidebar.shMain sidebar controller. Builds the session list, dispatches actions, and loops until the user closes the sidebar.
scripts/actions/switch-session.shSwitches the invoking tmux client to the selected session.
scripts/actions/create-project-session.shPicks a project directory, derives a name, creates a project-backed session, records metadata, and switches to it.
scripts/actions/create-adhoc-session.shCreates a non-project session by explicit name.
scripts/actions/rename-session.shRenames the chosen session with duplicate-name checks.
scripts/actions/kill-session.shKills a selected session after confirmation and safety checks.
scripts/lib/tmux.shShared helpers for reading tmux options, current client details, and formatted session data.
scripts/lib/projects.shShared helpers for enumerating project roots and normalizing directory names into default session names.
README.mdInstallation, configuration, keybindings, dependency notes, and manual test instructions.

The control path should pass explicit tmux context from the key binding into the scripts: the invoking client name, source window id, source pane id, and current path flow into open-sidebar.sh. After the split succeeds, the new sidebar pane id should be passed into scripts/sidebar.sh and into every action script. That keeps session switches, pane cleanup, and later verification tied to explicit targets rather than whichever pane or client happens to be active when a command runs.

The sidebar pane should be marked with a pane-scoped user option such as @session-sidebar-pane. That gives open-sidebar.sh a reliable way to find an existing sidebar in the current window without guessing from pane titles or commands.

Configuration and state

Configuration should live in tmux user options so that users can manage it directly in ~/.tmux.conf. The plugin should not introduce a separate config file in v1.

OptionMeaningSuggested default
@session-sidebar-keyToggle key bound after the tmux prefix.B
@session-sidebar-widthSidebar width passed to the split command.30%
@session-sidebar-project-rootsColon-separated directories searched by the project picker./home/brice/projects
@session-sidebar-use-fzfWhether to prefer fzf when it is installed.on
@session-sidebar-close-after-switchWhether the controller should exit after a successful session switch.on

Sessions created by the plugin may also store light session-scoped metadata:

  • @session-sidebar-kind = project or adhoc
  • @session-sidebar-project-path = absolute project directory for project-backed sessions

All create and rename paths should share one validation rule for session names. V1 should reject empty names, normalize predictable user-facing separators consistently, and keep every later tmux -t ... call pointed at explicit quoted variables rather than ad-hoc string interpolation.

Live runtime state should still come from tmux itself. Session lists, current-session markers, attached-state markers, and the target client are read on demand. V1 should not keep a separate cache or daemonized state layer.

Error handling and edge cases

  • Missing fzf: fall back to tmux prompts and simpler session selection instead of aborting.
  • Duplicate session on create: switch to the existing session by default and show a message rather than inventing hidden suffixes.
  • Duplicate session on rename: reject the rename, display a clear tmux message, and keep the sidebar open so the user can retry.
  • Last remaining session: refuse to kill it from the sidebar in v1 to avoid surprising detach behavior.
  • Current-client semantics: always target the invoking client so one user action does not move other attached clients unexpectedly.
  • Zoomed windows: accept tmux's native behavior rather than layering custom zoom restoration logic into v1.
  • Duplicate sidebars: detect the pane marker and avoid opening more than one sidebar in the same window.

Testing and verification

V1 should be verified primarily as a shell plugin plus tmux integration, not as a large software system.

  • Install through TPM in a local tmux config and confirm the entry script loads correctly. Also keep a direct source-file path available for isolated debugging.
  • Run shell quality checks such as shellcheck and formatting checks such as shfmt on the scripts.
  • Manually verify open, close, and toggle behavior in normal and already-open windows.
  • Manually verify switching sessions from the sidebar keeps the same terminal client attached, including a case where another client is attached elsewhere and should not move.
  • Manually verify project-backed session creation from /home/brice/projects, including predictable session-name normalization and collision-to-switch behavior.
  • Manually verify ad-hoc session creation, rename, duplicate-name handling, and kill confirmation.
  • Manually verify fallback behavior by setting @session-sidebar-use-fzf off, and if possible also by testing on a machine without fzf.

Future extensions

Once the v1 workflow is stable, later versions can explore a stickier sidebar by combining the current design with tmux hooks such as client-session-changed. That work should be treated as a separate iteration because it increases layout complexity and failure modes.

  • Auto-reopen the sidebar after session switches.
  • Show recent projects, tags, or project labels in the list.
  • Add session templates or project bootstrap commands.
  • Expose richer visual status, previews, or tree navigation.
  • Support optional saved layouts and restore helpers.
